**Club Deportivo Tenerife, S.A.D.** is a [Spanish football club](/wiki/List_of_football_clubs_in_Spain "List of football clubs in Spain") based in [Santa Cruz de Tenerife](/wiki/Santa_Cruz_de_Tenerife "Santa Cruz de Tenerife"), [Tenerife](/wiki/Tenerife "Tenerife"), in the [Canary Islands](/wiki/Canary_Islands "Canary Islands"). Founded in 1922, the club plays in the [Primera Federación](/wiki/Primera_Federaci%C3%B3n "Primera Federación") – Group 1, holding home matches at the [Estadio Heliodoro Rodríguez López](/wiki/Estadio_Heliodoro_Rodr%C3%ADguez_L%C3%B3pez "Estadio Heliodoro Rodríguez López"), with a 22,824-seat capacity. The traditional home colours are white shirts and blue shorts.

Tenerife has a history playing in the top flight of [La Liga](/wiki/La_Liga "La Liga"). They have been promoted to the top tier on four occasions, including a 10-year stint from [1989](/wiki/1989%E2%80%9390_La_Liga "1989–90 La Liga") to [1999](/wiki/1998%E2%80%9399_La_Liga "1998–99 La Liga"). The club managed to finish as high as fifth in the league table on two occasions during that period, which qualified them for the first round of the [UEFA Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Cup "UEFA Cup"). They most recently played in La Liga in the [2009–10](/wiki/2009%E2%80%9310_La_Liga "2009–10 La Liga") season.

Being based in the Canary archipelago off the Atlantic coast of Africa, while playing its away games on the Spanish mainland, both the club and rival [Las Palmas](/wiki/UD_Las_Palmas "UD Las Palmas") from [Gran Canaria](/wiki/Gran_Canaria "Gran Canaria") are two of the most geographically isolated professional clubs. Tenerife and Las Palmas contest the [Canary Islands derby](/wiki/Canary_Islands_derby "Canary Islands derby").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CD_Tenerife&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/Capit%C3%A3es_trocando_galhardetes_-_Antigo_Campo_dos_Barreiros%2C_1925.jpg/250px-Capit%C3%A3es_trocando_galhardetes_-_Antigo_Campo_dos_Barreiros%2C_1925.jpg)](/wiki/File:Capit%C3%A3es_trocando_galhardetes_-_Antigo_Campo_dos_Barreiros,_1925.jpg)

Match between [CD Nacional](/wiki/C.D._Nacional "C.D. Nacional") of Madeira and CD Tenerife in 1925.

Club Deportivo Tenerife was founded in 1922. [La Liga](/wiki/La_Liga "La Liga") started in 1928, but the team played in regional divisions until it was promoted to the [Segunda División](/wiki/Segunda_Divisi%C3%B3n "Segunda División") in 1953. It first reached the top flight in 1961, [being immediately relegated back](/wiki/1961%E2%80%9362_La_Liga "1961–62 La Liga") and, in the following 27 years, played almost exclusively in the second level, also spending three years in [Tercera División](/wiki/Tercera_Divisi%C3%B3n "Tercera División") and six – five in a row – in [Segunda División B](/wiki/Segunda_Divisi%C3%B3n_B "Segunda División B"), the newly created division three (in 1978).

In 1985 when Tenerife were relegated to the third division for a second time, Javier Pérez became president of the club. The side was promoted this year to the second level and, [two years later](/wiki/1988%E2%80%9389_Segunda_Divisi%C3%B3n "1988–89 Segunda División"), returned to the first, after winning the promotion playoff against [Real Betis](/wiki/Real_Betis "Real Betis") (4–1 on aggregate).

In 1991, [Jorge Valdano](/wiki/Jorge_Valdano "Jorge Valdano") took charge of the club as manager, and the Argentine would help deny former side [Real Madrid](/wiki/Real_Madrid_CF "Real Madrid CF") of two consecutive league titles in the last round, to the benefit of [Barcelona](/wiki/FC_Barcelona "FC Barcelona"). In [the first season](/wiki/1991%E2%80%9392_La_Liga "1991–92 La Liga"), the Canary Islands outfit barely avoided relegation, but would [finish in a best-ever fifth position in the following year](/wiki/1992%E2%80%9393_La_Liga "1992–93 La Liga"), eventually reaching the [round of 16](/wiki/Single-elimination_tournament "Single-elimination tournament") in [the subsequent](/wiki/1993%E2%80%9394_UEFA_Cup "1993–94 UEFA Cup") [UEFA Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Europa_League "UEFA Europa League"), losing to [Juventus](/wiki/Juventus_FC "Juventus FC") 2–4 on aggregate.

German [Jupp Heynckes](/wiki/Jupp_Heynckes "Jupp Heynckes") became head coach of Tenerife in 1995, [leading the club to another fifth-placed finish](/wiki/1995%E2%80%9396_CD_Tenerife_season "1995–96 CD Tenerife season") and the quarter-finals of the [Copa del Rey](/wiki/Copa_del_Rey "Copa del Rey"). In the [1996–97 UEFA Cup](/wiki/1996%E2%80%9397_UEFA_Cup "1996–97 UEFA Cup"), the islanders fared better, reaching the last-four after defeating [Maccabi Tel Aviv](/wiki/Maccabi_Tel_Aviv_F.C. "Maccabi Tel Aviv F.C."), [Lazio](/wiki/S.S._Lazio "S.S. Lazio"), [Feyenoord](/wiki/Feyenoord "Feyenoord") and [Brøndby](/wiki/Br%C3%B8ndby_IF "Brøndby IF") (the winner coming late in [extra time](/wiki/Overtime_(sports) "Overtime (sports)") from an [Antonio Mata](/wiki/Antonio_Mata "Antonio Mata") [free-kick](/wiki/Direct_free_kick "Direct free kick")), only bowing out to eventual winners [Schalke 04](/wiki/FC_Schalke_04 "FC Schalke 04").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/76/Club_Deportivo_Tenerife_league_performance_1929-present.svg/250px-Club_Deportivo_Tenerife_league_performance_1929-present.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Club_Deportivo_Tenerife_league_performance_1929-present.svg)

Club Deportivo Tenerife league performance 1929–present.

Tenerife then went on a downward spiral which eventually led to relegation to the "silver category" in [1999](/wiki/1998%E2%80%9399_La_Liga "1998–99 La Liga"), prompting various managerial changes within the club. In [2001](/wiki/2000%E2%80%9301_Segunda_Divisi%C3%B3n "2000–01 Segunda División"), the club was again promoted, led by [Rafael Benítez](/wiki/Rafael_Ben%C3%ADtez "Rafael Benítez"), who promptly left to take up the manager's job at [Valencia](/wiki/Valencia_CF "Valencia CF"); the promotion was achieved in the last match of the campaign thanks to a goal from [Hugo Morales](/wiki/Hugo_Morales_(footballer) "Hugo Morales (footballer)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/TenerifeRealSociedad08.jpg/250px-TenerifeRealSociedad08.jpg)](/wiki/File:TenerifeRealSociedad08.jpg)

Match: Tenerife – [Real Sociedad](/wiki/Real_Sociedad "Real Sociedad"), in 2008

[Pepe Mel](/wiki/Pepe_Mel "Pepe Mel") became the new trainer but the first division season never took off, as Tenerife were beaten heavily at home by Barcelona 0–6, which cost the manager his job. [Javier Clemente](/wiki/Javier_Clemente "Javier Clemente"), formerly with the [Spain national team](/wiki/Spain_national_football_team "Spain national football team"), took the reins, but could not help prevent [the eventual immediate relegation](/wiki/2001%E2%80%9302_La_Liga "2001–02 La Liga").

Tenerife suffered from serious economic problems in the following years, owing more than €40 million. President Pérez was replaced with [Víctor Perez de Ascanio](/w/index.php?title=V%C3%ADctor_Perez_de_Ascanio&action=edit&redlink=1 "Víctor Perez de Ascanio (page does not exist)"), who resigned due to bad management, leaving his position to [Miguel Concepción](/w/index.php?title=Miguel_Concepci%C3%B3n&action=edit&redlink=1 "Miguel Concepción (page does not exist)"), who negotiated with local politicians and businessmen, also creating a construction company as a subsidiary of the side.

On 13 June 2009, Tenerife [secured a top flight return](/wiki/2008%E2%80%9309_Segunda_Divisi%C3%B3n "2008–09 Segunda División") after a seven-year absence after a 1–0 win at [Girona](/wiki/Girona_FC "Girona FC"). In [the following season](/wiki/2009%E2%80%9310_La_Liga "2009–10 La Liga"), even though the team held on until the last round, another relegation befell, after the 0–1 loss at third-placed Valencia.

[2010–11](/wiki/2010%E2%80%9311_Segunda_Divisi%C3%B3n "2010–11 Segunda División") brought with it three coaching changes,[[2]](#cite_note-2) as Tenerife eventually suffered another relegation, returning to the third division after 24 years. On 2 June 2013, the club, led by [Álvaro Cervera](/wiki/%C3%81lvaro_Cervera "Álvaro Cervera"), returned to the second level after [winning the promotion play-off](/wiki/2013_Segunda_Divisi%C3%B3n_B_play-offs "2013 Segunda División B play-offs") against [Hospitalet](/wiki/CE_L%27Hospitalet "CE L'Hospitalet") (3–2 on aggregate).

Tenerife almost achieved promotion to La Liga in [2016-17](/wiki/2016-17_Segunda_Divisi%C3%B3n "2016-17 Segunda División") and [2021-22 season](/wiki/2021-22_Segunda_Divisi%C3%B3n "2021-22 Segunda División"), but was defeated by both [Getafe](/wiki/Getafe_CF "Getafe CF") and [Girona](/wiki/Girona_FC "Girona FC") at final play-off promotion in their respective season. Tenerife eventually suffered relegation to third division in [2024-25 season](/wiki/2024%E2%80%9325_Segunda_Divisi%C3%B3n "2024–25 Segunda División"), following a 12-years stay in second division.

## Fans

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CD_Tenerife&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Fans")]

Fans of Tenerife are called *Chicharreros* because in early days, the inhabitants of a small fishing village called [Santa Cruz](/wiki/Santa_Cruz_de_Tenerife "Santa Cruz de Tenerife") (later the capital of Tenerife) consumed "chicharros" ([Atlantic horse mackerel](/wiki/Atlantic_horse_mackerel "Atlantic horse mackerel")) as a main part of their diet.

Other inhabitants of Tenerife and the Canary Islands used the moniker as a pejorative name, but finally the inhabitants of Santa Cruz accepted it affectionately.
